name: Arched portal
description: An arched stone or brick portal that frames the front door of a building, transforming a plain wall opening into a ceremonial threshold. The arch can be round (Romanesque cathedral, Mediterranean villa), pointed (Gothic cathedral, monastery), or horseshoe (Moorish mosque iwan, Andalusian palace), and is typically recessed one to three blocks into the facade so the doorway sits inside a shallow vestibule cut by the thickness of the wall. The portal reads as a hierarchical transition: the outer arch is the public announcement, the recess creates pause and shadow, and the inner door is the private boundary. Often topped by a tympanum panel for sculpted ornament or an inscribed lintel; the surrounding jambs may carry columns, voussoirs, or moulded trim to amplify the sense of monumental entry.
placement_rules: Centre the portal on the principal facade so it dominates the approach axis; off-centre placement reads as a service door, not a ceremonial entry.

Recess the doorway one to three blocks into the wall thickness so the arch frames a shallow vestibule of shadow — this depth is what distinguishes a portal from a flat doorway with a decorative arch glued on.

Choose the arch profile from the style: round (semicircle, height = half the span) for Romanesque and Renaissance, pointed (two arcs meeting at an apex, height > half the span) for Gothic, horseshoe (arc extends below the springline) for Moorish-Mediterranean.
Keep the arch height-to-width ratio between 1.0 (round, height equals width) and 1.5 (steep pointed Gothic); ratios outside this range stop reading as a portal arch.

Choose single or double door leaves inside the portal: monumental buildings (cathedrals, palaces) use double doors with a central mullion; residential portals use a single door centred under the arch.
Reserve the tympanum — the semicircular or pointed panel above the door lintel and below the arch — for optional sculpted ornament, inscription, or a small rose window; leave plain in austere styles.
Match the portal voussoir material to the facade wall (or use a slightly lighter accent) so the portal reads as carved into the wall rather than bolted on.
lighting_ventilation: The recessed portal admits less direct light than a flat door because the vestibule shadow falls across the threshold; pair with wall-mounted lanterns or sconces inside the recess to mark the entry at night. The arch itself is solid masonry and does not ventilate — airflow comes through the door leaves when open. In sunny Mediterranean and Moorish variants the deep shadow of the recess is a deliberate climatic feature, cooling the threshold before one steps inside.

gothic.notes: Pointed arch with chiseled stone-brick voussoirs and a dark-oak double door; tympanum left plain or carrying a small chiseled inscription panel — reads as a cathedral or monastery portal.

mediterranean.notes: Horseshoe or round arch in smooth sandstone with chiseled-sandstone voussoirs; spruce door deeply recessed into a shaded vestibule — Andalusian villa or Moorish iwan.

renaissance.notes: Round arch in polished andesite with quartz keystone and tympanum; oak door framed by symmetrical pilasters — reads as a Florentine palazzo or Roman civic building.
